metadata.title: Why Electrons Jump Instead of Slide

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Heat a tube of hydrogen gas and it glows — but not with a rainbow. Only specific colors appear. Four in the visible range. The atom is broadcasting a fingerprint, and each line comes from one allowed electron jump in a surprisingly simple formula.

card.copy: Why Electrons Jump Instead of Slide

beats.narration_text: Classically, an electron orbiting a nucleus should radiate continuously, losing energy gradually and spiraling inward while emitting a continuous spectrum. This doesn't happen. The spectrum is discrete — sharp lines, not a rainbow.

production_viz.label: Classical prediction: continuous spectrum rainbow vs actual hydrogen: four sharp discrete lines

beats.narration_text: Why does heated hydrogen emit only four specific visible wavelengths — not a continuous rainbow? What determines which colors are allowed?

card.copy: Hydrogen glows with only four visible colors. Not a rainbow. Why are only specific wavelengths allowed?

beats.narration_text: Electrons occupy discrete energy levels. The energy of level n is minus 13.6 electron volts divided by n squared. Only specific values of n are allowed: 1, 2, 3, and so on. Electrons cannot exist between levels.

production_viz.label: Energy level ladder: E_n = -13.6/n² eV with rungs labeled n=1,2,3,4 — discrete levels, no continuum

beats.narration_text: When an electron drops from a higher level n_high to a lower level n_low, it emits exactly one photon. The photon's energy equals the difference: 13.6 times (1 over n_low squared minus 1 over n_high squared). The wavelength follows from E equals hc over lambda.

production_viz.label: Electron dropping between levels emitting single photon — energy gap formula and E=hc/λ connection

beats.narration_text: Electron dropping from n equals 3 to n equals 1: energy gap is 12.09 electron volts. Wavelength: 102.6 nanometers. Ultraviolet — invisible. This is the Lyman series.

production_viz.label: n=3→1 transition: 12.09 eV → 102.6 nm UV Lyman series — below visible range, shown in spectrum

beats.narration_text: Electron dropping from n equals 3 to n equals 2: energy gap is 1.89 electron volts. Wavelength: 656 nanometers. Red light — visible. This is hydrogen-alpha, the most prominent line in stellar spectra. From n equals 4 to n equals 2: 486 nanometers, blue-green, hydrogen-beta.

production_viz.label: Balmer series: n=3→2: 1.89 eV, 656 nm red Hα; n=4→2: 486 nm blue-green Hβ — visible spectrum lines

beats.narration_text: The Balmer series — transitions to n equals 2 — produces the four visible lines. Two in the red range, one blue-green, one violet. These are the fingerprint of hydrogen, identifiable anywhere in the universe.

production_viz.label: Hydrogen emission spectrum: four Balmer visible lines annotated — red, blue-green, violet, deep violet

beats.narration_text: Every element has its own unique energy levels and therefore its own unique fingerprint. Astronomers use spectral lines to identify the composition of stars and galaxies at any distance — the same discrete jumps, identified by the same formula.

production_viz.label: Spectral fingerprinting — element-specific line patterns used to identify stellar composition at any distance

beats.narration_text: E_n equals minus 13.6 electron volts over n squared. Drop n equals 3 to n equals 1: 12.09 electron volts, 102 nanometers UV. Drop n equals 3 to n equals 2: 1.89 electron volts, 656 nanometers red hydrogen-alpha. Drop n equals 4 to n equals 2: 486 nanometers blue-green hydrogen-beta. Only specific jumps allowed — only specific colors.

card.copy: E_n = −13.6/n² eV. n=3→1: 12.09 eV, 102 nm UV. n=3→2: 1.89 eV, 656 nm red (Hα). n=4→2: 486 nm blue-green (Hβ). Discrete levels → discrete spectrum → atom fingerprint.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
